refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 8.76 hours downtime per year) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation systems. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime / total period; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of network stack and teleoperation gateway to log link health events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics to the Safety Services module.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual-path network architecture (primary + backup) with automatic failover at the teleoperation gateway; monitor link health via heartbeat messages; log availability metrics.
DP-B: Implement redundant communication channels (cellular + satellite + wired) with load-balancing and seamless handoff; continuous health monitoring and real-time availability reporting.
DP-C: Implement a single high-reliability network path with extensive error correction and retransmission logic; rely on network provider SLA guarantees.
DP-D: Implement multi-layer redundancy (network, gateway, operator station) with active-active failover and distributed health monitoring across all layers.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Implement redundant communication channels (cellular + satellite + wired) with load-balancing and seamless handoff; continuous health monitoring and real-time availability reporting.

potential_sources: DP-B couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure selection (cellular, satellite, wired providers) and to the Safety Services module (for alert propagation on link loss).
DP-B decouples from specific vehicle control logic; the link layer is independent of what commands/telemetry traverse it.
DP-B introduces dependency on external network providers; mitigation requires contractual SLAs and fallback mechanisms.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is preferred over DP-C (single path) because it provides architectural redundancy; preferred over DP-A (dual-path only) because it adds satellite/wired diversity; preferred over DP-D (multi-layer) because it balances complexity and achievability. Coupling to Safety Services is acceptable and necessary for high-criticality operations.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥ 30 days to allow statistical confidence in 0.999 measurement.
Assumption: Network providers (cellular, satellite, wired) can contractually guarantee component SLAs that combine to ≥ 0.999.
Risk: Seamless handoff between channels may introduce transient link interruptions (milliseconds); mitigation requires sub-100ms failover logic.
Risk: Satellite latency (500+ ms) may degrade operator experience; mitigation requires operator training and vehicle autonomy features.
Risk: Cost of triple-redundancy (cellular + satellite + wired) may exceed budget; mitigation requires trade-study with customer.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to specify: (1) failover decision logic (e.g., link quality thresholds triggering switchover), (2) heartbeat interval and timeout values, (3) availability measurement methodology (e.g., 1-minute rolling window), (4) integration points with Safety Services for alert propagation on link loss.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link, including: (1) multi-channel network abstraction layer (cellular, satellite, wired), (2) failover and load-balancing logic, (3) link health monitoring and heartbeat generation, (4) availability metrics collection and reporting, (5) graceful degradation on link loss. The module interfaces with Vehicle (for command injection and telemetry reception), Safety Services (for link-loss alerts), and external Network Link providers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from the tele-operator station; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, diagnostics) from Vehicle to tele-operator station; Teleoperation module encodes and routes via redundant channels.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Notifies Safety Services of link loss events, failover events, and availability degradation; enables safety-critical responses (e.g., vehicle immobilization, operator notification).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network providers (cellular, satellite, wired); Teleoperation module manages multi-path routing, failover, and health monitoring across all available links.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. All failover, health monitoring, and redundancy management are contained within this module. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services, but the alert generation logic resides in Teleoperation.

Safety Services should NOT own link availability; it is a consumer of link-loss alerts. Assigning DP-B to Safety Services would scatter network redundancy logic across modules and violate separation of concerns.

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and telemetry generation, not network infrastructure. Assigning link availability to Vehicle would couple vehicle dynamics to network architecture, violating modularity.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; it should not own detailed link availability logic. This level is appropriate for system-level requirements allocation, not implementation of redundancy mechanisms.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-B (redundant communication channels with failover). It has clear ownership of the command-telemetry link, direct interfaces to Vehicle and Safety Services, and no competing responsibilities. Containment score 0.95 reflects minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns operator UI, command parsing, and telemetry visualization. Mitigation: Ensure clear separation between link-layer (owned by Teleoperation) and application-layer (owned by tele-operator station, external to NGHE).

Risk: Network provider integration (cellular, satellite, wired APIs) may introduce external dependencies that are difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: Define a Network Link abstraction interface to isolate provider-specific logic.

Risk: Failover logic must be deterministic and testable; non-deterministic behavior could cause intermittent link flapping. Mitigation: Specify failover decision thresholds and hysteresis to prevent oscillation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly define: (1) Teleoperation module boundary (link layer only, excluding operator UI), (2) Network Link interface contract (heartbeat, failover signals, availability metrics), (3) SafetyAlert message format and trigger conditions, (4) testing strategy for multi-channel failover scenarios.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (redundant communication channels with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve 0.999 link availability). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach: multi-channel architecture, load-balancing, seamless handoff, and continuous monitoring. The DP is implementable and measurable. No uncoupling or missing logic detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Failover decision logic may require coordination with Vehicle module (e.g., vehicle must gracefully handle command delays during failover). Mitigation: Define command timeout and retry semantics in Tasking interface contract.

Diffusion Risk 2: Availability metrics reporting requires integration with Safety Services and potentially external monitoring systems. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert and Telemetry interface contracts to include availability metadata.

Diffusion Risk 3: Network provider integration (cellular, satellite, wired) introduces external dependencies. Mitigation: Encapsulate provider-specific logic behind Network Link abstraction interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module containment score 0.95 is strong. The module owns the link layer, interfaces clearly with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and external Network Link providers. No significant diffusion detected. Ownership boundary is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of 0.999 availability is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state transitions during the evaluation period. Teleoperation module can instrument heartbeat messages, failover events, and channel health metrics. Availability calculation is straightforward: uptime / total period. Requires test environment with simulated network faults (latency, packet loss, channel failure) to validate failover behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns link availability. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) are allocated to Teleoperation with clear ownership. Safety Services owns the response to link-loss alerts (FR-NGHE-010.4 integration point). Vehicle owns graceful handling of command delays during failover. No ambiguity in allocation.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Refine FR-NGHE-010 to specify evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days) and measurement methodology (e.g., 1-minute rolling window, cumulative uptime).
Revision 2: Refine DP-B to specify failover decision thresholds (e.g., link quality metric < X triggers switchover), heartbeat interval (e.g., 100 ms), and timeout values (e.g., 500 ms).
Revision 3: Define Network Link abstraction interface contract, including heartbeat message format, failover signal semantics, and availability metrics schema.
Revision 4: Define SafetyAlert message format for link-loss events, including severity levels and recommended vehicle responses (e.g., immobilization, operator notification).
Revision 5: Establish test strategy for multi-channel failover scenarios, including simulated network faults and availability measurement validation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing failover detection, failover execution, network redundancy, and metrics reporting. DP-B is sound and well-coupled to the refined FR. Teleoperation module is the correct owner with strong containment (0.95). However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) require further decomposition to define concrete design parameters for failover logic, health monitoring, and metrics collection. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve atomic, implementable requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate failover or graceful degradation upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The network infrastructure shall provide redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics to the Safety Services module.

assumptions: Assumption 1: Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥ 30 days to allow statistical confidence in 0.999 measurement.
Assumption 2: Network providers (cellular, satellite, wired) can contractually guarantee component SLAs that combine to ≥ 0.999.
Assumption 3: Seamless handoff between channels can be achieved with sub-100ms failover logic.
Assumption 4: Operator training and vehicle autonomy features can mitigate satellite latency (500+ ms) impact on operator experience.
Assumption 5: Cost of triple-redundancy (cellular + satellite + wired) is acceptable to the customer.
open_risks: Risk 1: Failover decision logic may introduce transient link interruptions (milliseconds); requires sub-100ms failover logic and operator training.
Risk 2: Satellite latency (500+ ms) may degrade operator experience; requires vehicle autonomy features and operator training.
Risk 3: Cost of triple-redundancy may exceed budget; requires trade-study with customer.
Risk 4: Network provider integration introduces external dependencies; requires contractual SLAs and fallback mechanisms.
Risk 5: Failover logic must be deterministic and testable; non-deterministic behavior could cause intermittent link flapping.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Network provider SLA documentation (cellular, satellite, wired) confirming component availability targets.
Evidence 2: Failover decision logic specification, including thresholds, hysteresis, and timeout values.
Evidence 3: Test plan for multi-channel failover scenarios, including simulated network faults and availability measurement validation.
Evidence 4: Operator training plan addressing satellite latency and failover behavior.
Evidence 5: Cost analysis of triple-redundancy architecture vs. customer budget.
